summary: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I first heard about a fire at the World Trade Center when Joan Kelly told me a plane had hit the top of one of the towers. From the fuel dock on Ellis Island, I saw the first tower burning and then witnessed the second plane crash into the other tower. Later, I saw both towers collapse, which was an incredibly emotional and frightening experience. The World Trade Center attacks changed everything that day and left us all in shock as we tried to understand what was happening.

I was working on Ellis Island on the morning of the September 11 attacks, taking school reservations when the news broke. We evacuated the buildings and gathered at the wall of honor on the island, trying to stay safe and figure out what to do next. Ellis Island became a triage center where injured people were brought, and I helped by creating a database of their information. Being stuck on Ellis Island that day was terrifying, especially with the chaos and uncertainty all around us.

From Ellis Island, I could see New York City covered in smoke after the attacks on the World Trade Center. Everyone was afraid to go into the city, especially Battery Park, because of the chaos and destruction. The city was essentially shut down with bridges and tunnels closed, leaving many of us stranded on the islands nearby. It was a surreal and frightening sight to see the skyline so devastated and the city in such turmoil.

During the chaos on Ellis Island, we saw a steamboat approaching and feared it was coming for us, but it turned out to be an NYPD boat. Later, the NYPD requested the database I helped create with the names and information of injured people brought to Ellis Island. Their presence was part of the emergency response efforts that day, helping to manage the situation and coordinate rescue and triage operations. The NYPD played a critical role in trying to maintain order amid the confusion.

Liberty Island was evacuated on the morning of the attacks, and many people from there were brought over to Ellis Island. I saw a boat overloaded with people from Liberty Island heading to New York, but I chose not to get on it because I feared going into the city with all the smoke and chaos. The evacuation of Liberty Island added to the confusion and made it clear how widespread the emergency was across the harbor area.

I heard that some people were evacuated from Ellis Island by boat to Floyd Bennett Field in Brooklyn, but I was not on that boat and got the information late. Floyd Bennett Field served as a landing point for evacuees during the emergency response after the attacks. It was part of the broader effort to move people safely away from the affected areas when bridges and tunnels were closed.

After the attacks, when it was finally safe to travel, the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge reopened, allowing us to get back to Brooklyn from Jersey City. I left Ellis Island around 6 p.m. and stayed at a friend's house until it was safe to go home. The reopening of the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge was a crucial step in restoring some normalcy and allowing people to return home after the chaos of the day.

The September 11 attacks were a day of confusion, fear, and chaos for everyone on Ellis Island and around New York Harbor. I witnessed the planes hitting the World Trade Center towers and saw both towers collapse, which was devastating. We were stuck on Ellis Island with no way to get home, and emergency responders brought injured people to the island for triage. The attacks changed everything that day and left a lasting impact on all of us who experienced it firsthand.

While we were on Ellis Island, we heard reports that the Pentagon had also been hit, which added to the shock and confusion of the day. The news made it clear that the attacks were coordinated and widespread, not just limited to New York City. This information heightened the sense of danger and uncertainty as we tried to figure out what was happening and how to stay safe.

The New Jersey State Police were involved in bringing injured people to Ellis Island for triage after the attacks. Their emergency services worked alongside other responders to manage the influx of people needing medical attention. Their presence was part of the coordinated effort across state lines to respond to the crisis and provide aid where it was most needed.
